response:
# DataTable (Sortable, Paginated, Per-Row Menus) Accessibility Testing Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver), keyboard-only users, low-vision users (200%/400% zoom) > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA + Chrome (primary), JAWS + Chrome/Edge, VoiceOver + Safari, keyboard-only **Feature:** Accessibility testing plan for an existing React DataTable — sortable column headers, Previous/Next pagination, and per-row Actions dropdown menus — flagged by engineering as a known accessibility risk. **Risk Level:** High (four independently complex subsystems — table semantics, sort state, pagination, per-row menus — that all interact with screen reader table mode and keyboard navigation). **Component Type:** Data Table (composite: native `` + sortable headers + pagination control + APG Menu Button per row). --- ## Scope & Context **What is being built/tested:** A repeatable accessibility test plan for an existing custom React `DataTable` component. The component is not being designed from scratch — it exists and is suspected of having defects. The plan must (a) define the correct accessible behavior precisely enough that a QA engineer can recognize a defect, and (b) script the tests to detect deviations. **Why this plan exists:** Engineering has flagged the component as a known accessibility risk. The four subsystems each have well-documented failure modes (table semantics that don't associate headers, sort state that updates visually but not programmatically, pagination that loses focus, menus that don't trap/restore focus). The QA team that will run this rotates and has only basic accessibility knowledge — so the plan must be self-contained, with exact key sequences, exact expected announcements, and measurable pass/fail criteria. A test that says "verify the table is navigable" is useless to a rotating team; "press Ctrl+Alt+Right and confirm NVDA announces the column header before the cell value" is executable by anyone. **Who needs accessibility here:** - **Screen reader users** — the highest-risk population for data tables. Table semantics, header association, sort announcements, and menu focus management are all invisible to sighted testing and all break silently. - **Keyboard-only users** — must reach every sortable header, every pagination control, and every row menu item, and must never get trapped. - **Low-vision users** — depend on column headers staying visible at 200%/400% zoom and on horizontal scroll being keyboard-operable. **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA. The criteria most load-bearing for this component: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, 4.1.2 Name/Role/Value, 4.1.3 Status Messages, 2.1.1 Keyboard, 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap, 2.4.3 Focus Order, 2.4.7 Focus Visible, 1.4.10 Reflow, 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (for the menu), 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum). **Assistive technologies to support:** NVDA + Chrome is the primary, mandated test combination. JAWS + Chrome/Edge and VoiceOver + Safari are secondary confirmation passes. Keyboard-only is mandatory on every test. The plan front-loads NVDA + Chrome and treats JAWS/VoiceOver as confirmation rather than primary discovery — matching the team's stated constraint. **What exists / what this modifies:** The DataTable React component already exists. This is a verification plan against existing code, so the plan's structure stubs describe the *expected* DOM the tests assert against — they are the test oracle, not a build spec. Where the stub and the rendered DOM diverge, that divergence is the defect. **Constraints that shape the plan:** - Complex interaction model (sort + pagination + per-row menus) — the plan sequences tests so that a failure in foundational semantics is caught before time is spent on dependent behaviors. - Team has limited axe-core experience — the automated section names exact rule IDs and explains what each catches and what it cannot, so testers don't over-trust a green axe run. - Tests maintained by a rotating QA team — every manual test specifies exact keys and exact expected output; Playwright assertions are concrete (attribute names, expected values) so a new tester can read and extend them. - NVDA + Chrome primary — NVDA table-mode commands are documented in full; JAWS/VoiceOver equivalents are provided in a single reference table rather than repeated per test. **Negative space — what this plan does NOT cover:** It does not test the backend sort/pagination correctness (whether the *right* rows come back is a functional test, not an a11y test — though the plan does verify the announced sort state matches the visible state). It does not cover bulk-select/row-selection checkboxes (not in the spec). It does not cover column resizing or column reordering (not in the spec). It does not address data export. If any of these exist in the real component, they are out of scope for this plan and need their own. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan This section defines the test oracle: the DOM the component is expected to render. QA asserts against this; deviations are defects. **Landmark / document context:** The table lives inside the dashboard's `
` landmark. The table itself is not a landmark. If the component wraps the table in a region for the scroll container (see Reflow below), that wrapper takes `role="region"` with an accessible name and `tabindex="0"` so keyboard users can scroll it — but the table semantics must NOT be disturbed by the wrapper. **Heading context:** The table has a `
`, not a heading, as its accessible name. The caption text is "Transaction Records — 742 results". (If a visible `

` precedes the table in the page, that's fine, but the caption is what associates a name with the table element per WCAG 1.3.1.) **Expected table structure stub (test oracle — not implementation code):** ```
← scroll container, keyboard-focusable ← not sortable, plain text ← row identifier as row header (see note) … 49 more rows …
Transaction Records — 742 results
← sortable DescriptionCategory ← sortable ← sortable Account Actions
2026-06-12Coffee shop purchase Dining $4.50 Posted Checking ••1234
Sorted by Date, ascending
Page 2 of 15 ← see Pagination announcement note
``` **Key semantic decisions and their citations:** 1. **`

` for all 7 column headers** — WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships. This is the spec's stated structure and is correct for a flat, single-level header table. Each data cell is associated with its column header by the browser's table algorithm via `scope="col"`. 2. **`scope` is sufficient — do NOT add `headers="..."` to data cells.** For a single-level column-header table (and one row header per row), `scope="col"` / `scope="row"` fully expresses the relationships. The `headers` attribute is only required for irregular/multi-level header tables (spanning headers, split headers). Prescribing `headers` on all 350 `` cells of a flat 7-column table is over-engineering, creates a maintenance burden, and is a known planning smell. **Test the scope attributes; do not require headers attributes.** (If the rendered component happens to use `headers`/`id` and they are *correct and complete*, that's acceptable — but a missing-or-partial `headers` set is worse than none, so the plan's acceptance criterion is "scope attributes correct," not "headers attributes present.") 3. **First column (Date) as `` — RECOMMENDED, verify against actual render.** A row header lets screen readers announce the row's identifying value when the user navigates across that row's cells, which dramatically improves comprehension ("$4.50, Coffee shop purchase" makes more sense than "$4.50" alone). The spec's stub shows `` only and is silent on row headers. **The plan tests for this as a recommendation (MEDIUM severity if absent), not a hard failure** — many production tables use all `` and remain WCAG-conformant. If the team adds a row header, Date is the natural choice (it's the first column and reasonably identifying). Flag, don't block. 4. **Sortable header = ``, with `aria-sort` on the ``.** Two separate, correct facts that QA must verify *together*: (a) the interactive control is a real `` element, NOT on the button, because `aria-sort` is only valid on a cell/columnheader/rowheader role. A frequent defect is `aria-sort` placed on the `
` and an `aria-label`/`aria-labelledby` are present on the ``, the ARIA wins and the caption may be dropped from the accessible name — axe rule `table-duplicate-name` flags this collision. Test: the table's accessible name is "Transaction Records — 742 results" and comes from the caption, with no competing `aria-label` on the `
`. --- ## Interaction Pattern Design Every interactive element mapped to its APG pattern, with the keyboard model and ARIA the tests assert against. ### Interactive Elements Table | Widget | APG Pattern | Keyboard | ARIA (on which element) | WCAG | |--------|-------------|----------|--------------------------|------| | Sortable column header (Date, Amount, Status) | [Sortable Table Column Header](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/table/examples/sortable-table/) — `
`; `aria-sort` on `` | Tab to button; **Enter** or **Space** toggles sort | `` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2, 2.1.1 | | Sort announcement | [Live Region](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/practices/live-regions/) | n/a (announced on sort) | `aria-live="polite"` on a single dedicated region | 4.1.3 | | Previous / Next pagination | [Button](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/button/) inside a `
` | Tab to button; **Enter**/**Space** activates | native `
` and small icon buttons are common places where a focus ring is clipped by `overflow:hidden` on the cell or table. **Test:** Tab to each control type and confirm a visible ring; specifically check the Actions buttons (icon buttons in a possibly-overflow-clipped cell) and the scroll region. **8. Horizontal scroll is keyboard-operable** — WCAG 2.1.1, 1.4.10. The scroll container is focusable (`tabindex="0"`, `role="region"`, `aria-label`) so keyboard users can scroll it with Arrow keys to reach off-screen columns (Account, Actions on a narrow viewport). A scroll container that only responds to mouse drag / trackpad is a keyboard trap of information. **Test:** Tab to the region → press Right Arrow repeatedly → confirm the table scrolls and the Actions column becomes reachable. --- ## State Communication Design Every state, communicated both visually and programmatically, with the ARIA the test asserts. ### State Communication Table | State | Visual indicator | Programmatic indicator | ARIA (element) | WCAG | |-------|------------------|------------------------|----------------|------| | Column not sorted | neutral / no glyph | `aria-sort="none"` (or attribute absent) on `` | `aria-sort="none"` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Column sorted ascending | ▲ glyph (aria-hidden) | `aria-sort="ascending"` on `` | `aria-sort="ascending"` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Column sorted descending | ▼ glyph (aria-hidden) | `aria-sort="descending"` on `` | `aria-sort="descending"` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Sort just changed | rows reorder; glyph updates | live-region text update | `aria-live="polite"` region: "Sorted by Date, ascending" | 4.1.3 | | Menu collapsed | button only; chevron/dots | `aria-expanded="false"` on trigger | `aria-expanded="false"` | 4.1.2 | | Menu expanded | menu visible below button | `aria-expanded="true"`; focus in menu | `aria-expanded="true"`, `role="menu"` | 4.1.2 | | Page changed | new rows; "Page N of M" updates | live-region text update | `aria-live="polite"` (pagination region) | 4.1.3 | | Previous disabled (page 1) | greyed button | not focusable / announced disabled | `disabled` attribute | 4.1.2 | | Next disabled (last page) | greyed button | not focusable / announced disabled | `disabled` attribute | 4.1.2 | | Loading new page (if async) | spinner / skeleton | busy state announced or deferred announce | `aria-busy="true"` on table region while loading, then page-change announce on settle | 4.1.3 | **`aria-sort` cycle — the central state test.** Only ONE column may have a non-`none` `aria-sort` value at a time (sorting by Amount must reset Date to `none`). A defect where two columns simultaneously claim a sort direction misrepresents the data. **Test:** sort Date ascending → assert Date `aria-sort="ascending"` AND Amount/Status are `none` → sort Amount → assert Amount is now `ascending` AND Date reset to `none`. **Single sort glyph at a time, matching `aria-sort`.** The visual glyph and the `aria-sort` value must agree. A common defect is the glyph updating but `aria-sort` not (or vice versa), so a sighted user and a screen reader user perceive different states — WCAG 1.4.1 territory (information conveyed by the glyph must also be programmatic). **Test:** assert that the column showing the ▲ glyph is the same column whose `` has `aria-sort="ascending"`. **Live-region timing.** The live region must already exist in the DOM at page load (empty), and only its *text content* changes on sort. Injecting a brand-new element containing the message at sort time is unreliable — many screen readers won't announce a region that didn't exist when they built their buffer. **Test (DOM):** confirm the `aria-live` region is present on initial render and that sorting mutates its text rather than inserting a new node. **Pagination announcement — design decision.** Two acceptable approaches; the plan accepts either but tests whichever the component uses: - (a) The "Page N of M" indicator is itself an `aria-live="polite"` region whose text changes on page change. Simple; works. - (b) A separate visually-hidden live region announces "Page 3 of 15, showing results 101 to 150." More informative. What is NOT acceptable: a "Page 3 of 15" that updates silently with no live region — a screen reader user who clicked Next then hears nothing and doesn't know the page changed (focus stayed on Next, so there's no focus-move announcement to cue them). **Test:** click Next → assert the pagination live region's text updated to reflect the new page. --- ## Visual Accessibility Plan **Color contrast — WCAG 1.4.3.** Body text and header text ≥ 4.5:1 against their backgrounds (including any zebra-striping background — test BOTH the white-row and striped-row backgrounds). Sort glyphs and the focus ring as non-text UI ≥ 3:1 per WCAG 1.4.11. axe-core covers text contrast; the glyph and focus-ring contrast need a manual/contrast-tool check. **Test:** run axe `color-contrast`; manually sample the sort glyph and focus ring against both row backgrounds. **Color not the sole sort indicator — WCAG 1.4.1.** Sort direction must not be conveyed by color alone (e.g., a blue-tinted header). It is conveyed by the ▲/▼ glyph (shape/position) AND by `aria-sort` (programmatic). Status column values ("Posted," "Pending," "Failed") must likewise not rely on color alone — each status needs text (it does, since the cell contains the word) and, if a colored dot/badge is used, the text label must be present. **Test:** view in grayscale — sort direction and status are still distinguishable by glyph/text. **Focus indicator technique — WCAG 2.4.7.** A two-color focus indicator (outline + box-shadow, or outline + offset) so the ring is visible on both the header background and on white/striped rows. Use `:focus-visible`. Watch for the indicator being clipped by `overflow` on the ``, the cell, or the scroll region — a clipped ring reads as "no focus indicator." **Test:** Tab through; confirm a fully-visible ring on every control type, including icon buttons in cells. **Reflow / zoom — WCAG 1.4.10.** At 400% zoom (≈320 CSS px wide), the *page* must not require two-dimensional scrolling. The *table* may scroll horizontally — that's the permitted exception for data tables (1.4.10 explicitly exempts content requiring 2D layout, which large tables do). The critical requirements: (a) **column headers remain visible/associated** while scrolling — the header row must scroll *with* the body or stick, so a user at 400% who scrolls to the Amount column still gets "Amount" announced; (b) **no functionality is lost** at narrow width — every column, every Actions menu, and pagination remain reachable; (c) the rest of the page (outside the table region) reflows to one column with no horizontal page scroll. **Test:** set browser to 1280px and zoom 400% (or DevTools 320px width); confirm the *page* has no horizontal scrollbar, the *table region* does, the header row stays put/visible, and Tab still reaches every control. **200% zoom — WCAG 1.4.4.** At 200% the table text resizes (relative units) without content loss or overlap; column headers must not be lost. **Test:** browser zoom 200%; headers still present, no clipped/overlapping text. **Touch target size — WCAG 2.5.8.** The Actions button, sortable header buttons, and Previous/Next must be ≥ 24×24 CSS px (2.5.8 minimum) — ideally 44×44. Icon-only Actions buttons packed into dense table rows are the likely violator. **Test:** measure the Actions button and pagination button hit areas. **Reduced motion — WCAG 2.3.3.** If row reordering on sort, or menu open, uses animation, it must respect `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`. Low-risk here, but check. **Test:** set OS reduced-motion; confirm no large motion on sort/menu. --- ## Content Accessibility Plan **Caption — WCAG 1.3.1.** The `
` "Transaction Records — 742 results" names the table and gives the total count (useful: a screen reader user paging through 50-row pages knows there are 742 total). Verify the count in the caption matches reality / updates if filtered (if filtering exists — out of declared scope, but if present, a stale "742" is misleading). **Actions button accessible names — WCAG 4.1.2, 2.4.4.** As above: each Actions button's name disambiguates its row (`aria-label="Actions for {row identifier}"`). Without this, 50 identically-named buttons are unusable. **Use `aria-label`, not `title`.** **Menu item labels — WCAG 2.4.4.** "Edit," "Delete," "View Details" are clear out of context. If the design later shortens these to icons, they'll need accessible names — not a current issue but noted. **Sortable header text — WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels.** Header buttons read as their column name ("Date," "Amount," "Status"). The aria-hidden glyph must not bleed into the accessible name (confirm the glyph is `aria-hidden`, so the button name is exactly "Date," not "Date ▲" or "Date black up-pointing triangle"). **Test (DOM):** the computed accessible name of the Date header button is "Date". **Empty / decorative cells — WCAG 1.1.1.** If any cell renders an icon (e.g., a status icon), decorative icons get `aria-hidden`/`alt=""`; meaningful icons get a text alternative. The Actions "⋯" glyph is decorative (the button is named by `aria-label`), so the glyph itself must be `aria-hidden` so it isn't announced as "three dots / horizontal ellipsis" on top of the label. **Reading order — WCAG 1.3.2.** DOM order matches visual order (header row first, then body rows top-to-bottom, cells left-to-right). React table libraries that virtualize rows can desync DOM order from visual order — if virtualization is in use, verify DOM order tracks visual order. (Virtualization also interacts with screen reader row counting — see SR section.) **Language — WCAG 3.1.1.** Inherited from the page ``; nothing table-specific unless cells contain other-language content. --- ## Testing Strategy This is the core deliverable. It is organized so a rotating QA engineer can execute top-to-bottom, and so foundational failures are caught before dependent tests waste time. ### A. Automated Testing (axe-core + Playwright) — run FIRST **Why first:** automated tests are fast, deterministic, and catch the table-semantics failures that everything else depends on. They are also the part the team is least experienced with, so the rules are named explicitly with what they do and do not catch. **Setup:** - Install `@axe-core/playwright`. Run axe against the table on **every state variant**, not just default: (1) initial load, (2) after sorting Date ascending, (3) after sorting Date descending, (4) after sorting Amount, (5) on page 2, (6) on the last page, (7) with a row menu open. State changes can introduce/remove DOM that axe only sees in that state. - Scope axe to the table region where possible to keep findings focused, but run at least one full-page pass to catch `table-duplicate-name` and landmark issues. **axe-core rules that matter for this table (name them, don't run blind):** | Rule ID | What it catches | Why it matters here | |---------|-----------------|---------------------| | `scope-attr-valid` | `scope` has a valid value (col/row/colgroup/rowgroup) | Verifies the `scope="col"` headers are valid | | `th-has-data-cells` | every `
` is associated with ≥1 data cell | Catches an orphaned header (e.g., a header for a removed column) | | `td-headers-attr` | IF `headers` is used, it references real IDs in the same table | Only fires if the component uses `headers`; catches broken/partial `headers` sets (the reason we prefer scope) | | `table-duplicate-name` | table doesn't have both a caption and a conflicting aria-label as its name | Catches caption-vs-aria-label name collision | | `empty-table-header` | header cells aren't empty | Catches a sortable header whose text got swallowed by markup | | `color-contrast` | text meets 4.5:1 (3:1 large) | Header + body + striped-row text | | `aria-required-attr` / `aria-valid-attr-value` | ARIA attributes valid and complete | Catches `aria-sort` with an invalid value, or a menu missing required ARIA | | `button-name` | every button has an accessible name | Catches an unlabeled Actions "⋯" button | | `aria-allowed-attr` | ARIA attributes are allowed on their element | **Catches `aria-sort` on a ``** | **What axe-core CANNOT catch (state this to the team so a green run isn't over-trusted):** - Whether the `aria-sort` *value* is correct for the actual sort direction (axe checks validity, not correctness). - Whether the live region actually *announces* on sort/page change. - Whether focus moves/restores correctly (sort focus retention, menu focus restoration, pagination focus). - Whether NVDA reads column headers per cell in table mode. - Whether the menu's keyboard model (arrows, Esc) works. - Whether headers stay visible at 400% zoom. These all require the manual + scripted-interaction tests below. **A clean axe run means the static semantics are likely sound — it says nothing about the four interactive subsystems.** **Playwright scripted interaction tests (deterministic, CI-runnable, maintainable by the rotating team):** These assert the things axe can't — attribute *values* and live-region *content* after interaction. Pseudocode (the team fills in selectors): ``` test('sort cycle on Date header sets aria-sort none→ascending→descending→none'): load table dateTh = th containing button "Date" expect(dateTh) aria-sort == "none" (or absent) click dateButton expect(dateTh) aria-sort == "ascending" expect(otherSortableThs) aria-sort == "none" ← only one sorted at a time expect(liveRegion).toContainText("Sorted by Date, ascending") click dateButton expect(dateTh) aria-sort == "descending" expect(liveRegion).toContainText("Sorted by Date, descending") click dateButton expect(dateTh) aria-sort == "none" ← completes the cycle expect(activeElement) == dateButton ← focus retained on sort test('aria-sort lives on the , not the

_benchmark.method: plan 006 Phase D Claude lane: Agent(subagent_type=a11y-planner, model=opus), one background subagent per fixture; verbatim PLANNER_PROMPT_PREFIX task; agent wrote plan via Write tool; orchestrator wrapped to response JSON
